name: Sara Guzzell
goals:
She wants to do quick checks on the house without stopping what she's doing. A single glance at her wrist to confirm the door is locked or the stove is off is all she needs before running out the door for a 12-hour shift.
She wants the house to switch into "Night Mode" the second she gets home, lights dim, alarm on, heating adjusted, without her having to touch her phone after an exhausting day.
She wants to only be notified when something actually matters. A water leak or an unlocked door at 2 AM is worth a buzz on her wrist. A "system update available" notification is not.
frustrations:
She hates it when a simple task like turning off a bedside lamp requires opening an app, navigating a menu, and waiting for it to load. If it takes more than two taps, she'd rather just get up and use the physical switch.
She gets really frustrated when her watch buzzes constantly with irrelevant system updates or stats she didn't ask for. After a while, she just starts ignoring all notifications, which means she misses the important ones too.
She feels like a guest in her own home when the settings are too locked down or technical. Having to ask Sebi to change the living room lighting mood makes her feel like she has no real control over her own space.
scenarios: Sara is already halfway down the stairs, heading to a night shift, when she realizes she might have left the living room lights on. Instead of going back up, she glances at her watch, sees the "Lights On" status icon, and taps it once to turn them off. She feels a quick haptic confirmation on her wrist and keeps walking without breaking her stride.
otherDetails: Her driving force is peace of mind. She doesn't care how the system works under the hood, she just needs to trust that it's doing its job so she can focus on hers. For her, a good smart home is one she forgets exists.
